title: Revealing Wavelength- and Size-Dependent CO2 Reduction Selectivity via Operando Scanning Photo-Electrochemical Microscopy
authors: Fatemeh Kiani, Milad Sabzehparvar, Priscila Vensaus, Elif Nur Dayi, Olga D'Anania, Tarique Anwar, Nuria Lopez, Ravishankar Sundararaman, Giulia Tagliabue
abstract: Controlling product selectivity in plasmonic catalysis, particularly in CO2 reduction (CO2R), remains a central unsolved challenge with direct implications for light-driven fuel and chemical synthesis. Here, we deploy quantitative operando scanning photoelectrochemical microscopy (photo-SECM) to provide a direct demonstration that tuning photon energy switches CO2R selectivity through an electronically driven pathway. On plasmonic Au/p-GaN photocathodes, interband excitation (460-560 nm) drives selective CO production while intraband excitation (640-800 nm) favors H2 evolution. By maintaining constant absorbed power across wavelengths and confirming linear power dependence, we isolate the role of hot-carrier energy from photonic and photothermal contributions. Density functional theory calculations reveal that higher-energy interband excitation progressively increases the overlap between hot-electron-accessible states and the CO-producing intermediate, selectively promoting CO over formate, in excellent agreement with experiment. We further show that selectivity is geometrically gated by hot-carrier transport: sub-100 nm nanostructures sustain CO2R activity, while ~300 nm nanodisks suffer transport losses that suppress it, consistent with ab initio hot-carrier transport calculations. Together, these results establish photon energy, carrier transport, and nanostructure geometry as coupled design parameters for plasmonic CO2R selectivity, resolve a longstanding debate on the origin of plasmon-driven selectivity effects, and position photo-SECM as a broadly applicable operando platform for photo(electro)catalysis.
summary: This work uses operando photo-SECM on Au/p-GaN photocathodes to show that CO2 reduction selectivity is highly tunable by changing the incident photon energy and catalyst size. The mechanism is determined to be electronically driven, where high-energy hot carriers promote CO formation, while particle size dictates efficient carrier transport necessary for optimal performance.
main_problem: The central challenge is controlling product selectivity during plasmon-driven CO2 reduction (CO2R) in photocatalysis, which is crucial for light-driven fuel synthesis.
main_result: Selectivity is governed by a coupled interplay between the incident photon energy (interband vs. intraband excitation), the resulting hot-carrier energy distribution, and the nanostructure geometry of the catalyst.
method: The study employs operando Scanning Photo-Electrochemical Microscopy (photo-SECM) to measure product selectivity in situ, complemented by Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations for theoretical validation.
model_or_system: The system uses Au nanostructures deposited on p-GaN photocathodes. The mechanism relies on generating hot electrons via photon absorption, which drive the selective reduction of CO2 over competing reactions like HER.
key_observables: Product selectivity (CO vs. H2), Faradaic Efficiencies (FEs) as a function of wavelength and power, and spatially resolved hot-carrier energy fluxes.
important_parameters: Photon energy regimes (interband: 460-560 nm; intraband: 640-800 nm); nanostructure size (<100 nm vs. ~300 nm); constant absorbed power to isolate hot-carrier effects.
main_assumptions: The selectivity switch is confirmed to be driven by the electronic nature of hot carriers, not merely photothermal heating; transport losses are significant in larger nanostructures.
figures_summary: Figures demonstrate wavelength-dependent product yields (CO vs. H2) using photo-SECM measurements and show theoretical comparisons between hot-carrier fluxes in small vs. large Au nanostructures.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the problem, uses photo-SECM to observe spectral/size dependence of selectivity, explains this dependence via DFT modeling linking high-energy carriers to CO formation, and finally confirms the role of carrier transport losses based on particle size.
why_interesting: While primarily in electrocatalysis, the detailed analysis of hot-carrier energy transfer, state filling (DOS overlap), and geometry-dependent charge transport provides a rich model for understanding non-equilibrium electron dynamics relevant to open quantum systems or strongly coupled plasmonic environments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
